coredata.dc:title: Heterogeneous carbon dot catalysts for biodiesel production: A mini review
coredata.dc:creator: Tracey, Chantal T.
Shavronskaya, Darya O.
Shao, Jing'ai
Yang, Haiping
Krivoshapkin, Pavel V.
Krivoshapkina, Elena F.
coredata.dc:description: Biodiesel is a petroleum diesel substitute made by reacting oils and fats obtained from plants and animals with short-chain alcohols. The most common approach to biodiesel synthesis is transesterification, which, although a spontaneous reaction, is often catalysed by both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts. Homogeneous catalysts, typically very strong acids and bases, have the disadvantages of requiring special equipment and being very difficult to separate from the end product; therefore, heterogeneous catalysts, primarily metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, are preferred. However, conventional heterogeneous catalysts have several limitations, including poor industrial applicability. Recently, carbon-based nanoparticles, including carbon dots (C-dots), have been proposed as potential catalysts. A fledgling innovation, C-dots are a promising heterogeneous catalyst due to their easy, cost-effective synthesis; modifiability; high specific surface area; high specificity; and high conversion efficiency. Furthermore, unlike other catalysts, C-dots do not have to be removed after biodiesel production and can be included as an additive that improves fuel and engine performance and reduces greenhouse gas emission. This mini review aims to highlight the emerging role of C-dots in biodiesel synthesis via transesterification.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different types of carbon dots. Carbon dots are classified as carbon polymer dots (CPDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) depending on type of carbon hybridisation, degree of carbonisation, degree of crystallinity, type of crosslinking, and surface functionalisation.

Commonly used top-down and bottom-up approaches to C-dot synthesis. C-dots are obtained by breaking down large carbon structures using methods like laser ablation and arc discharge (top) or through the aggregation of small organic molecules or polymers using synthetic procedures like pyrolysis and solvothermal/hydrothermal synthesis (bottom).

C-dot heteroatom doping and functionalisation. Heteroatoms like nitrogen, phosphorus, sulphur, zinc, and copper can be incorporated into the C-dot structure during synthesis or added to the C-dot surface after synthesis to improve their physicochemical properties. Nitrogen- and oxygen-containing surface functional groups can also be grafted to the C-dot surface after synthesis.

Publication trends of C-dot applications over time. A – four primary applications of C-dots; B – number of articles published on fluorescence, nanodots, and carbon nanoparticles over time; C – publications trends of various sensing, optics, biomedical, and catalytic applications of C-dots over time.

Mechanism of action for carbon dot-catalysed transesterification. Alkaline-catalysed transesterification mechanism of action (top). Due to amine and carboxyl functional groups on the C-dot surface, they can be categorised as weak or non-ionic bases and have similar mechanisms of action where (i) surface carboxylate groups act as a proton acceptor for the short-lived zwitterion that forms after the alcohol attacks the oil or (ii) surface amine groups first deprotonate the alcohol, which then attacks the oil (bottom).
Advantages and disadvantages of various carbon-based nanocatalysts in biodiesel production.

list-item.para: can be produced in industrial quantities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodiesel is a petroleum diesel substitute made by reacting oils and fats obtained from plants and animals with short-chain alcohols. The most common approach to biodiesel synthesis is transesterification, which, although a spontaneous reaction, is often catalysed by both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts. Homogeneous catalysts, typically very strong acids and bases, have the disadvantages of requiring special equipment and being very difficult to separate from the end product; therefore, heterogeneous catalysts, primarily metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, are preferred. However, conventional heterogeneous catalysts have several limitations, including poor industrial applicability. Recently, carbon-based nanoparticles, including carbon dots (C-dots), have been proposed as potential catalysts. A fledgling innovation, C-dots are a promising heterogeneous catalyst due to their easy, cost-effective synthesis; modifiability; high specific surface area; high specificity; and high conversion efficiency. Furthermore, unlike other catalysts, C-dots do not have to be removed after biodiesel production and can be included as an additive that improves fuel and engine performance and reduces greenhouse gas emission. This mini review aims to highlight the emerging role of C-dots in biodiesel synthesis via transesterification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their discovery, biodiesels have proven to be an invaluable source of renewable energy. Monoalkyl esters of long chain fatty acids, biodiesels are biodegradable manmade fuels manufactured from vegetable oils or animal fats [1]. Often used as environmentally friendly additives to or complete substitutes for petrodiesel derived from fossil fuels, these plant- and animal-derived biofuels have been shown to increase overall energy security, improve air quality, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions and are generally safer for the environment [2]. Biodiesels have a wide range of applications, from powering vehicles, providing heat, and generating electricity to lesser known uses such as cleaning grease and oil spills and as engine lubricants [3]; therefore, biodiesels are an indispensable commodity.

While several synthetic methods exist [4], biodiesels are most commonly obtained via transesterification, during which fats and oils obtained from plants and animals are reacted with short-chain alcohols to produce alkyl esters [5]. This is because, unlike other conventional biodiesel synthesis methods (i.e., pyrolysis, dilution/blending, and micro-emulsification), transesterification occurs at relatively low temperatures and pressures and produces neat biofuels that chemically resemble petrodiesel [4]. Although transesterification is spontaneous under heat and/or pressure, it is a slow process; as a result, the reaction is often catalysed. Throughout the years, different catalysts have been successfully utilised to accelerate the transesterification process, such as lipases [6] and homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts. Although lipase-catalysed transesterification has the highest yield of all the known synthetic methods, it is rarely used because of the long reaction time and high cost associated with it [6]. Similarly, homogeneous catalysts (i.e., strong acids and bases) are not the first choice due to several limitations, including the need for corrosion-resistant equipment, difficulty in separating the catalyst from the final product after the reaction is completed, and the presence of unwanted impurities [7]. To overcome these disadvantages, most transesterification reactions are catalysed by heterogeneous catalysts.

Heterogeneous catalysts, which are typically metals and metal oxides like titanium dioxide, strontium oxide, and various calcium oxides [8–13], are a relatively cheap yet effective means of accelerating transesterification. Heterogeneous catalysts have several innate properties that make them more appropriate for transesterification than lipases and homogeneous catalysts, including high solubisation; enhanced catalytic activity and specificity; high thermal stability; and easy separation from the product, which results in relatively high product purity [4,11,12]. Lately, researchers have explored taking these metals and metal oxide heterogeneous catalysts to the nanoscale. Studies have shown that this increases their catalytic activity and specificity due to an increase in specific surface area and surface energy [14–16].

Although the most suitable of the conventional catalysts, nanometals and nanometal oxides still possess several limitations that negatively impact transesterification efficiency, including time inefficiency, low efficiency, mass-transfer resistance, high catalyst loading, and poor industrial applicability [4,17]. Furthermore, metallic nanocatalysts tend to be expensive and prone to leaching, which prevent the large-scale industrial production of biodiesel using these catalysts [18]. Another major disadvantage of metallic nanocatalysts is their fast deactivation due to coke formation, which occurs when the nanometal/nanometal oxide reacts with the hydrocarbons being used in the reaction, resulting in nonoptimal biodiesel yield [19]. To circumvent these issues, researchers have proposed the use of carbon-based heterogeneous nanocatalysts [17,19].

Like their metallic counterparts, carbon-based nanocatalysts have been shown to have high specific surface area, high catalytic activity, and high reactivity; however, they also possess high selectivity and specificity, exhibit good efficiency, and significantly reduce reaction time [17]. Over the past few decades, carbon-based nanoparticles have proven to be highly effective heterogeneous catalysts. In addition to their excellent mechanical and support properties, they have surfaces that can be modified with various functional groups and heteroatoms can easily be incorporated into their structure, which can expand their application potential; they exhibit inertness towards unwanted reactions and high stability under regeneration and reaction conditions; and they can be obtained in various shapes and sizes to suit different chemical reactor configurations [20]. Furthermore, unlike metallic nanocatalysts, carbon-based nanocatalysts show high resistance toward coke formation as well as low affinity for coke [9,19]; as such, they do not react with the long hydrocarbon chains of the fatty acids and monoalkyl esters and remain catalytically active, increasing biodiesel yield. Carbon-based nanoparticles also have distinctive optical, electron transfer, and structural properties that manifest as unique physicochemical properties, such as good thermal and electrical conductivity; high thermo-, chemo-, photo-, and mechanical stability; and light absorptivity, which makes them excellent catalysts [21]. Thus far, several studies have shown the ability of carbon-based nanomaterials to quickly and successfully catalyse biodiesel production, including carbon nanohorns [22,23], carbon nanotubes [24,25], and carbon nanofibres [26]. More recently, however, researchers have begun to explore the viability of carbon dots (C-dots).

In this mini review, we aim to spotlight the emerging role of C-dots in biodiesel production and justify their potential large-scale application and adaptation. In section two, we explore the various types of C-dots; describe their synthesis, heteroatom doping, and functionalisation; explore their optical and electron transfer properties; and underscore their suitability as a green technology. Next, we highlight how the properties of these carbon-based nanomaterials make them suitable transesterification catalysts and present several examples of C-dots catalysing biodiesel production in section three. Section four summarises the current state of this field and offers our perspectives on the future of this approach to transesterification. Finally, in the conclusion, we summarise the main arguments in support of using C-dots in biodiesel production.

The papers analysed in this review were found using Google Scholar as well as the Web of Science, Scopus, PubMed, ScienceDirect, and IEEE Xplore academic research databases using the keywords carbon dots, carbon dot optical properties, carbon dot electron transfer properties, catalysis, carbon dot catalysis, biodiesel production, transesterification, heterogeneous catalysts, catalytic carbon nanomaterials, green technology, and fuel additives. Additionally, SciVal was used to visualise publication trends and to determine the level of scientific interest in this research area. A total of 93 relevant references were cited in this review. The term ‘carbon dots in biodiesel production’ was then used to find real-world examples of C-dots being used to catalyse transesterification. Here, a total of five papers that highlighted the use of C-dots as co-catalysts or sole catalysts for biodiesel production were found. With the exception of one that was published in 2016, all examples were published between 2019 and 2023, indicating a burgeoning interest in the use of C-dots as heterogeneous catalysts in transesterification for biodiesel production.

C-dots are optically active, fluorescent zero-dimensional quasi-spherical carbon-based nanomaterials consisting of an aromatic/aliphatic core and surface functional groups [27]. C-dots are typically less than 10 nm in size; however, C-dots as large as 60 nm have been reported [28,29]. A core comprised of sp2 and sp3 hybridised carbon atoms with various functional groups and/or polymer chains attached to their surfaces, C-dots is the umbrella term for several zero-dimensional amorphous or graphitic carbon-based materials, namely carbonised polymer dots (CPDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) – depending on the type of carbon hybridisation, degree of carbonisation, degree of crystallinity, type of crosslinking, and functional groups/polymer chains present on the surface of the aromatic/aliphatic core (Fig. 1 ) [29,30]. A comprehensive review on the structural nuances of the various types of C-dots can be found here [31]. C-dot structure depends on several factors, including synthetic method, heteroatom doping, and surface functionalisation.

C-dots are obtained via two main synthetic approaches: top-down and bottom-up synthesis [31]. During top-down synthesis (Fig. 2 , top), large carbon structures are broken down into carbon structures less than 10 nm in size [32]. In contrast, during bottom-up synthesis (Fig. 2, bottom), C-dots are constructed from small organic molecules [32]. Conventional synthetic approaches encompass several chemical, physical, and electrochemical methods such as (electro)chemical exfoliation, which, while fast, efficient, scalable, and provides high-quality end products, requires toxic organic chemicals [33] as well as laser ablation, nanometre etching, and arc-discharge, which, although simple and time-efficient methods, are in themselves expensive and have high energy consumption [33–36]. Furthermore, these methods usually use inorganic carbon sources like high-purity graphite powders, carbon nanofibres, and carbon nanotubes, which can be quite expensive, as well as costly equipment and hazardous chemicals, making such approaches to C-dot synthesis costly and unsustainable [33,36]. Fortunately, recent studies have shown that C-dots can be cheaply and sustainably synthesised from inexpensive ecologically friendly carbon sources.

The use of renewable natural organic sources enables the low-cost, large-scale, and sustainable production of many materials, helps reduce waste, and improves carbon neutrality, which is vital for their widespread and industrial application and adoption [37]. Such an approach to material synthesis also has broad implications for green technology as a whole because of the development of new green synthesis methods and minimal environmental impact. These green synthetic methods are designed to be more energy efficient, involve safer chemicals and auxiliaries (some of which have to be specially developed), and use renewable feedstocks. This leads to less hazardous chemical synthesis and inherently safer chemistry for accident prevention [38]. Furthermore, the end products are usually biodegradable and nontoxic, leading to reduced pollution upon disposal. An added benefit of using natural organic sources is that they are incredibly versatile and can be used as feedstock for several materials, including C-dots [38].

Using green technology, C-dots can be economically and sustainably synthesised from natural organic carbon sources, which is a major advantage. These natural carbon sources include highly replenishable and abundant biopolymers and biomolecules obtained from plants and animals, which are usually cheaper, easier, and safer to obtain than other carbon sources [37]. Additionally, the synthetic methods used to obtain C-dots from these biomaterials often require very mild reaction conditions, making them more economically viable. Typical synthetic procedures include low-cost and time-efficient microwave- ultrasound-assisted methods like cavitation; most-often used solvothermal/hydrothermal synthesis, another low-cost synthetic strategy with the added advantage of producing nontoxic C-dots in the case of hydrothermal synthesis; and pyrolysis, a method that has short reaction times and is defined by low cost, easy operation, and scalable production. These synthetic methods tend to be simple one-step and/or one-pot procedures that take several minutes to a few hours [39]. Furthermore, most of these approaches to C-dot synthesis require simple, easily obtained, and/or common laboratory devices and equipment as well as standard non-hazardous laboratory reagents [36]. Detailed reviews on C-dot synthesis from environmentally friendly carbon sources can be found here [30,40].

While C-dots exhibit inherent exploitable chemical and physical characteristics, these properties often need to be enhanced for useful application. This is typically done through heteroatom doping or functionalisation (Fig. 3 ), which enables the physicochemical properties of C-dots to be fine-tuned, resulting in them exhibiting desirable characteristics like protracted chemical stability, good dispersibility in aqueous media, and increased catalytic activity [41].

Doping introduces heteroatoms into the C-dot structure, in a single-step procedure, to improve their quantum yield, water solubility, and, most importantly, fluorescent properties [42]. Here, atoms of various sizes and with different electronegativities (usually Groups III, V, and VI elements like boron, nitrogen, phosphorous, and sulphur) replace some carbon atoms in the core or surface functional groups during C-dot synthesis (Fig. 3). To date, nitrogen doping has proven to be the most effective, which can be attributed to its atomic size being comparable to that of carbon; therefore, it is the most-often introduced dopant.

Another way of enhancing C-dot properties is through surface functionalisation, a simple yet effective method of tuning the surface properties of a material, including surface area, number of active sites, and surface reactivity [33]. Surface functionalisation is achieved via multi-step synthesis (Fig. 3), where modifiers (e.g., functional groups, polymers, biomolecules, ions, and metals) are attached to previously synthesised C-dots via covalent and non-covalent interactions [43,44]. As shown in Fig. 1, C-dots have a multitude of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing moieties on their surface that can act as points of contact for modifiers [29,45]. The most common C-dot surface modifiers include boron, nitrogen, phosphorous, and sulphur as well as oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups (e.g., amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups). Less common modifiers include metal and metalloid dopants like zinc, manganese, silicon, and copper [43].
Both doping and surface functionalisation affect C-dot structure and surface properties, which in turn enhances their properties, making them desirable and useful materials.

Of their many useful physicochemical characteristics, C-dots are primarily known for their inextricably tied optical and electron transfer properties. Like many functionalised or defect-rich carbon-based nanomaterials, C-dots are fluorescent, absorbing energy from incident ultraviolet radiation and emitting light in the visible range of the electromagnetic spectrum. Although C-dots typically absorb ultraviolet radiation, C-dots that absorb in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum have been obtained [33,36]. Upon light absorption, electrons in the C-dot structure become excited; these excited electrons then return to a lower energy state through the emission of a photon. The absorbance and emission characteristics of C-dots can be manipulated through surface passivation, surface functionalisation, or doping, which results in increased absorption or a red-shift in their absorption and emission wavelengths [46]. C-dots usually exhibit excitation-dependent emission, which means that their emission wavelength depends on their excitation wavelength; however, C-dots with excitation-independent fluorescence have been obtained [30,47]. C-dots have also been shown to exhibit strong photoinduced electron transfer upon irradiation, wherein an excited electron is transferred from a donor to an acceptor, resulting in charge separation (the generation of an electron-hole pair) or fluorescence quenching (when the electron is transferred from donor to acceptor by way of a non-radiative path) [48].

Photoluminescence quantum yield, a property that indicates the efficiency of an optically active fluorescent material [49], is another exploitable optical characteristic of C-dots [30,47]. As with fluorescence, several factors influence this C-dot property, especially electron population, which itself can be manipulated through heteroatom doping and surface functionalisation, which introduces excess electrons or holes to the C-dot structure and/or electron-rich groups (e.g., amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl) to the C-dot surface [33]. C-dots exhibiting up-conversion photoluminescence, a phenomenon in which a material converts low-energy photons into high-energy photons [50], have also been synthesised [30,47]. Furthermore, C-dots show extreme stability and the ability to retain their colours upon exposure to ultraviolet and visible light – a rare property for fluorescent materials, which tend to lose colour with time when subjected to radiation for long periods [51].

As previously stated, the many useful physicochemical properties of C-dots are exploitable, which makes them highly sought out materials; however, it is their distinctive optical and electron transfer properties that make them excellent multifunctional materials [29,52], which has piqued scientific interest as of late [40]. Since their accidental discovery in 2004, C-dots have found various applications across many industries (Fig. 4
A) and, thus far, have primarily been used in optics, where their bright fluorescence, high photoluminescence quantum yield, and remarkable photostability allow them to complement and amplify the optical properties of other materials [53]; sensing, where they can be used to detect substances thanks to photoinduced electron transfer fluorescence quenching [54]; biomedicine, where, in addition to sensing and imaging, their many surface functional groups allow various moieties to be grafted onto their surface, making them excellent carriers [55]; and catalysis, where their charge generation and separation properties along with their electron conductivity enable them to speed up otherwise slow chemical reactions [56].

As shown in Fig. 4 B, scientific interest in C-dots has steadily increased since 2013. The most popular applications for C-dots so far have been as light-emitting diodes for lasers, in anti-counterfeiting, sensing, phototherapy, gene and drug delivery, and in catalysis (Fig. 4 C). Comprehensive reviews on applications of C-dots in optics, biomedicine, sensing, and catalysis can be found here [41,55,57,58].

As stated above, C-dots have been recognised as potent catalyst over the past few years, not only because of their optical and electron transfer characteristics, but also because of their modifiability through surface functionalisation and heteroatom doping. Regarding surface modification, functional groups found on the C-dot surface endow them with high specificity and selectiveness, making them highly efficient catalysts [29]. Furthermore, the introduction of excess electrons or holes upon heteroatom doping changes the internal electronic environment of C-dots, which in turn improves their catalytic activity and enhances their interactions with reactants [59].

Thus far, C-dots have been shown to exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity and have been used in carbon dioxide conversion, solar water splitting, and the photodegradation of organic compounds as both sole catalysts and as co-catalysts to metals and semiconductors [60]. The photocatalytic activity of a material strongly depends on its ability to separate photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transport them; thus an efficient catalyst would efficiently generate electron-hole pairs and delay their recombination. C-dots act as electron mediators and the relay of photoexcited electrons throughout their electron transfer network effectively retards electron-hole recombination, making them good photocatalysts [40].

C-dots have also proven to be effective electrocatalysts when used as electrode materials in hydrogen evolution, oxygen evolution, oxygen reduction, and carbon dioxide reduction reactions as well as in electrical energy storage [61,62]. Despite their size, C-dots provide large specific surface area and rapid electron transfer. During electrocatalysis, C-dots decrease band gap size, allowing for more efficient electron transportation and higher electric conductivity.

Inspired by other carbon-based nanoparticles (specifically carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, carbon nanohorns, graphene nanoparticles, and graphene oxide nanoparticles), C-dots have been proposed as potential heterogeneous nanocatalysts for the production of biodiesel via transesterification. Conventional carbon-based nanocatalysts possess several limitations which prevents their large-scale adoption and implementation, making them unsuitable for biodiesel production, including cost, difficult syntheses, poor dispersibility, hydrophobicity, susceptibility to surface defects, difficulty in surface functionalisation, and sometimes low conversion efficiency (Table 1 ). On the other hand, C-dots are easy and cheap to synthesise, highly dispersible, hydrophilic, and are easy to dope and functionalise [36,63], making them more suitable heterogeneous transesterification catalysts.

In one of the first examples, C-dots were used as co-catalysts in in-situ transesterification. Also known as reactive extraction, in-situ transesterification involves the direct production of fatty acid methyl esters from oil-bearing biomass, such as algae, by bringing the biomass in direct contact with an alcohol containing a catalyst [78]. Tangy et al. used C-dots as a strontium oxide (SrO) co-catalyst to produce biodiesel using fatty acids obtained directly from Chlorella vulgaris, single-celled green microalgae with a lipid content of ∼ 25 % [10,79]. SrO and ultrafine C-dots, obtained by sonicating polyethylene glycol, were combined to form a catalytically active nanocomposite that was 93 wt% SrO and 7 wt% C-dots. The resulting nanocatalyst was dispersed in methanol then introduced to C. vulgaris in chloroform. The system was then irradiated in a microwave oven equipped with a mixture at ∼ 60 °C for 2.5 min. The authors reported a lipid conversion yield of 97 wt%, which is more efficient than SrO, the most promising heterogeneous catalyst, being used as a sole catalyst. When only commercial SrO is used, the reaction time more than doubles to 6 min to achieve similar results.

In another example, Borah et al. paired GQDs with tungsten oxide (WO3) to convert waste cooking oil into biodiesel using methanol [80]. Like SrO, tungsten oxides are popular heterogeneous transesterification catalysts [81–85]. The GQDs were obtained via the electrolysis of citric acid dissolved in NaOH using graphitic rods. The resulting GQDs were introduced to a tungsten disulphide/n-methyl pyrrolidone and the subsequent mixture calcined to form the WO3/GQD system. This catalyst had a biodiesel yield of 96.69 % at 70 °C and a catalyst loading value of 2 wt% at a 1:6 oil-to-alcohol ratio after 3.5 h. This is more efficient than other tungsten oxide-based catalysts, which have lower biodiesel yields at similar temperatures, reaction times, and catalyst loading values and higher oil:alcohol ratios [85,86]. The resulting biodiesel satisfied international standards [80].

In a final example, Kalpana et al. obtained CQDs through the hydrothermal treatment of exopolysaccharides obtained from Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 and used them as co-catalysts in the base- and acid-catalysed transesterification of waste cooking oil with methanol [87]. Adding these CQDs to the reaction vessel increased biodiesel production when used as co-catalysts for both sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid; however, the authors found that CQDs worked best with the base, with a yield of 96.67 % as opposed to the 87.27 % yield obtained when paired with the acid. Qualitative analysis of the resulting biodiesel showed that biodiesel obtained from the transesterification of waste cooking oil with CQDs and NaOH meet international standards in terms of oxidative stability. The biofuel also had a high cetane number, indicating good ignition quality.

When used as co-catalysts, C-dots modulate or enhance the catalytic activity of both heterogeneous and homogeneous nanocatalysts during transesterification. C-dots work in conjunction with both types of conventional catalysts to accelerate the transesterification process, reduce catalyst loading value, increase reaction yield, and improve biodiesel quality.

More recently, however, C-dots have been used as the sole catalyst in biodiesel production. In one of the first examples of this, Macina et al. synthesised glycine-citric acid C-dots that were then used to catalyse the transesterification of canola oil with methanol [18]. Under an optimal catalyst loading value of 1.0 wt%, Macina et al. obtained a yield of 98.3 ± 0.2 % at 150 °C within 3 h at an oil:methanol ratio of 1:18. Thus far, two potential mechanisms of operation have been proposed (Fig. 5 ).

First, the authors hypothesised that C-dots behave similarly to homogeneous bases. In alkaline-catalysed transesterification, the strong base deprotonates the short-chain alcohol to form the conjugate base of the alcohol, which then attacks the carbonyl group of the oil [18]. Because of the many nitrogen-containing organic functional groups on the C-dot surface (i.e., amine groups), they can be classified as weak bases [88,89]. During transesterification, the amine surface functional groups deprotonate the alcohol to form the conjugate base that then attacks the oil. However, this mechanism is accompanied by an energy barrier, which can be overcome at high temperatures and pressures.

For the other potential mechanism, it was proposed that the alcohol attacks the oil directly to form a short-lived intermediate zwitterion, and carboxylate groups on the C-dot surface facilitate a proton transfer, shifting the reaction equilibrium [18]. The authors hypothesised that transesterification proceeded via both proposed mechanisms, wherein methanol directly attacked the canola oil and carboxylate groups on the C-dot surface facilitated a proton transfer, followed by normal transesterification.

In their most recent work, the same authors confirmed that carboxylate and amine groups on the C-dot surface act as active catalytic sites and that transesterification occurs by way of both mechanisms highlighted in Fig. 5 (bottom), depending on which type of functional group is more prevalent on the C-dot surface [67]. When more carboxylate groups are present, the C-dot nanocatalysts accept protons from the intermediate zwitterion formed when the short-chained alcohol attacks the ester, then traditional base-catalysed transesterification proceeds. Conversely, when amine groups dominate the C-dot surface, the nanocatalyst acts as a weak base that deprotonates the alcohol to form the conjugate base, which then attacks the ester. It must be noted that when more amine groups are present, transesterification requires higher reaction temperatures to achieve high biodiesel conversion. The research group also found that when carboxylate and amine groups are on the C-dot surface in comparable numbers, transesterification proceeds via both mechanisms in a competitive process that, unfortunately, decreases biodiesel yield. de Medeiros et al. also found that C-dots require a negative surface charge when catalysing the reaction as no biodiesel conversion occurs when the C-dot surface is positively charged [67].

As the examples suggest, C-dots are very effective heterogeneous catalysts for the conversion of plant and animal fats and oils into biodiesel via transesterification. Compared to those catalysed by conventional heterogeneous catalysts, transesterification reactions catalysed by C-dots have shorter reaction times, lower catalyst loading values, and high yields, which may be attributed to their extensive surface functionalisation, which results in very high specific surface area, a large number of active sites, and high specificity [10]. However, it must be noted that C-dot-catalysed reactions require higher temperatures (up to 150 °C) due to the presence of an intrinsic activation energy barrier [18]. While the temperature can be lowered to ∼ 100 °C, the reaction would need to occur at higher pressures [67]. Furthermore, like other heterogeneous nanocatalysts, C-dots require high oil:alcohol molar ratios because excess alcohol favours methyl ester formation [67]. However, the ratios have been lowered in the most recent work, from oil:alcohol ratios of 1:18 to 1:9, which was accomplished by controlling whether amine or carboxylate groups dominate the C-dot surface [67]. C-dot nanocatalysts also exhibit high cyclability, and retain their high efficiency for about five cycles [18,80].

Another major advantage of C-dots is that, when used as the sole catalyst, they do not have to be separated from the biodiesel end product and can be left in as an additive in biodiesel or biodiesel/petrodiesel blends. Diesel additives are substances that can be added to petro- or biodiesel to improve fuel combustion, enhance overall engine performance, and potentially reduce toxic emissions [90]. Several studies have shown that C-dots are suitable fuel additives and can be used to improve fuel combustion, improve engine performance, and reduce toxic emissions.

For example, Heidari-Maleni et al. added GQDs to ethanol-biodiesel blend that was then used in an internal combustion engine [91]. The authors observed that adding GQDs increased the power and torque of the engine by 12.42 % and 28.18 %, respectively; reduced specific fuel consumption by 14.35 %; and reduced carbon monoxide and unburned hydrocarbon emissions by approximately 30 % each, compared to petrodiesel. In a follow-up study, they investigated the effect of GQD concentration (in ppm) on ethanol-biodiesel blend performance [92]. Heidari-Maleni et al. added 30, 60, and 90 ppm GQD as additives to the ethanol-biodiesel blend and found that engine performance increased with increasing GQD concentration and, as expected, fuel containing 90 ppm GQDs had the best performance. Compared to pure petrodiesel and a 10 % biodiesel/petrodiesel mix, adding 90 ppm GQD to the ethanol-biodiesel blend increased power by 15.69 % and 21.84 %, respectively, and torque by 15.39 % and 16.26 %, respectively. The addition of 90 ppm GQDs also decreased specific fuel consumption by 17.58 % and 12.52 %, unburned hydrocarbons by 38.91 % and 25.13 %, and nitrogen oxide by 1.54 % and 0.25 %, respectively, when compared to petrodiesel and the 10 % biodiesel.

In another instance, Ettefaghi et al. obtained similar results when adding GQDs to several biodiesel/petrodiesel/water blends [93]. Like Heidari-Maleni et al., these authors also investigated the effect of GQD concentration. The results showed that engine power increased with increasing GQD concentration up to 60 ppm, after which no significant improvements were observed. For their blend containing 60 ppm GQDs, engine performance improved by 13.6 %, specific fuel consumption was reduced by 7.8 %, and reduced unburned hydrocarbon emissions by 29.2 % compared to petrodiesel. This group managed to quantify the decrease in nitrogen oxide and soot emissions, which were 3.8 % and 33.7 %, respectively. Ettefaghi et al. also found that surface functional groups play a pivotal role when GQDs are used as an additive [93]. While the presence of fewer oxygen-containing surface functional groups decreased nitrogen oxide emissions, it lowered engine power and increased soot and unburned hydrocarbon emissions. This was due to the partial removal of oxygenated functional groups increasing GQD surface area, which substantially increased GQD heat transfer capability, which in turn reduced engine thermal efficiency.

The oxygen-containing functional groups found on the C-dot surface compensates for the lack of oxygen in the combustion chamber, allowing for the fuel to mix better with air, leading to a better and more complete combustion process and a subsequent increase in engine power [93]. Better combustion was also found to decrease hydrocarbon and soot emissions. The studies also showed that oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the C-dot surface affects the heat transfer capabilities, causing a decrease in the local temperature of the combustion chamber and limiting the amount of nitrogen oxide emissions. It must be noted that there is a restriction on the number of oxygen- and nitrogen containing groups that can be on the C-dot surface, however, as having too many can have the opposite effect and increase nitrogen oxide emissions [93].

By catalysing transesterification using C-dot nanocatalysts, high quality biodiesel can be quickly and efficiently obtained. Furthermore, not only is there no need to remove the very small amounts of C-dots used in transesterification but leaving it in positively affects the overall performance of subsequent biodiesel/petrodiesel blends in terms of better engine performance and lower toxic emissions.

As discussed, C-dots show immense potential for application in biodiesel synthesis as they possess several distinct advantages. Depending on the carbon source and synthetic method, C-dots can be economically and sustainably obtained, which suggests that they have large-scale industrial applicability. The use of ecologically friendly natural organic carbon sources and the development of synthetic methodologies are vital to the green and sustainable production of C-dots. Biomass is cheaper, easier, and safer to obtain than other carbon sources, require very mild reaction conditions, and the resulting C-dots are biodegradable, biocompatible, and nontoxic. Paired with their excellent optical and electron transfer properties, C-dots have found application not only in catalysis, but also in biomedicine, sensing, and optics.

C-dots have high specific surface areas, indicating the availability of a large number of catalytic active sites for reaction and, therefore, high catalyst productivity. The surfaces of C-dots are also highly modifiable: the introduction of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups allows their surface properties, such as number of active sites, surface acidity, and surface basicity, to be modulated, which in turn affects transesterification reaction conditions like temperature and oil:alcohol ratio. The type and number of surface functional groups also increase catalyst specificity and determines the reaction mechanism. Additionally, C-dots show high specificity and high conversion efficiency for biodiesel production via transesterification, increasing the amount of biodiesel produced while reducing the amount of unwanted glycerol byproducts at very low catalyst loading values.

One of the main advantages of using of C-dots to catalyse transesterification is that, unlike conventional metallic heterogeneous catalysts, they do not react with the long-chain hydrocarbons of fatty acids during biodiesel synthesis or the fatty acid methyl ester end product to form coke, increasing reaction yield. Furthermore, carbon-based nanocatalysts have low affinity for coke, in case of its formation. This feature allows carbon-based nanoparticles to remain catalytically active throughout the reaction and reusable, since they are still viable catalysts. C-dots have been shown to be highly recyclable, plateauing at a conversion yield of 95 % after five transesterification cycles. Compared to conventional metallic heterogeneous catalysts, C-dot-catalysed transesterification usually requires higher temperatures and high oil:alcohol ratios. Fortunately, recent studies have shown that reaction temperatures can be lowered and the amount of alcohol needed to favour methyl ester formation can be drastically reduced by modulating the type of functional group on the C-dot surface. Carboxylate groups on the C-dot surface facilitate proton transfers that are needed after the alcohol attacks the oil and zwitterions are formed, lowering reaction temperature and oil:alcohol ratio.

Thus far, C-dots have been used as both sole catalysts and as co-catalysts to conventional transesterification catalysts. When used as co-catalysts, C-dots modulate or enhance the catalytic activities of both heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysts by shortening reaction time, reducing the catalyst loading value, increasing biodiesel yield, and improving biodiesel quality. When used as the sole catalyst, C-dots have similar effects; however, after the reaction ends, C-dots can be left in as an additive to improve the quality of the resulting biodiesel and overall engine performance while reducing toxic nitrogen oxide, hydrocarbon, and soot emissions.

In conclusion, C-dots are strong contenders as potential catalysts for the synthesis of biodiesel via transesterification not only in terms of the sustainability and cost-efficiency associated with their use, but also concerning the overall quality of the end product and their subsequent effect on engine performance and environmental friendliness when left in as additives. While there are still several limitations associated with using C-dots as heterogenous catalysts in biodiesel production, namely relatively high reaction temperatures and oil:alcohol ratios compared to homogeneous catalysts, recent studies have shown that they can be overcome. Despite this, the inherent advantages (i.e., short reaction times, low catalyst loading values, and high product yield) make them viable heterogeneous transesterification catalysts.
